front.title: Agentic Reasoning Protocol (ARP): DNS-Bound Cryptographic Verification for Machine-Readable Entity Claims

abstract.t: This document specifies the Agentic Reasoning Protocol (ARP), a lightweight mechanism for cryptographically binding machine-readable entity claims to domain ownership via DNS TXT records. ARP enables AI agents and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines to verify that structured data published on a domain was authorized by the domain owner, preventing narrative injection and entity spoofing in generative search systems.

ARP uses Ed25519 digital signatures (RFC 8032), JSON Canonicalization Scheme (JCS, RFC 8785), and DNS TXT records to establish a chain of trust analogous to DomainKeys Identified Mail (DKIM, RFC 6376) but designed specifically for the verification of semantic entity data consumed by autonomous AI agents.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: The proliferation of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Retrieval- Augmented Generation (RAG) systems has fundamentally altered how information is discovered, synthesized, and presented to users. Unlike traditional search engines, which return ranked lists of hyperlinks for human evaluation, generative search engines produce direct synthesized answers by extracting and compressing data from multiple web sources.

This architectural shift creates a critical trust gap: generative engines heavily weight structured data signals (JSON-LD, semantic HTML, Schema.org markup) when selecting sources for citation, but currently have no mechanism to verify that such structured data was actually authorized by the domain owner. Any actor can publish well-formed structured data on any domain they control, potentially poisoning the knowledge graph of AI systems with fabricated entity claims, false product information, or competitor sabotage.

The Agentic Reasoning Protocol (ARP) addresses this gap by providing a DNS-anchored cryptographic verification mechanism analogous to DKIM for email. ARP enables any consuming system to verify the provenance and integrity of machine-readable entity claims before incorporating them into synthesized responses.

dl.dd: Verification requires only a DNS TXT lookup and a single Ed25519 signature verification. No PKI infrastructure, certificate chains, or external services are required.
Trust is rooted in the existing global DNS infrastructure, leveraging the same anchor used by DKIM, SPF, and DMARC.
The protocol is designed for consumption by autonomous AI agents and automated crawlers, not human users.
The signed payload and verification metadata are co-located in a single JSON file, minimizing the number of network requests required for verification.
The protocol is fully specified in this document and requires no proprietary software or services.

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

dl.dd: The brand, organization, person, or concept described by an ARP payload.
Machine-readable self-attested statements published by the entity about itself, including identity metadata, factual corrections, domain expertise, and content policies.
A JSON document (conventionally named "reasoning.json") containing the entity claims and associated verification metadata. Served at the well-known URL path "/reasoning.json" on the entity's domain.
The party that holds the Ed25519 private key and produces the signature. This SHOULD be the domain owner or an entity explicitly authorized by the domain owner.
Any system that retrieves the reasoning payload and validates the signature against the DNS-published public key. Typical verifiers include AI agents, RAG pipelines, and search engine crawlers.
A label used to construct the DNS TXT record name, enabling multiple keys on the same domain (e.g., for key rotation or multi-tenant configurations).

section.name: Protocol Overview
section.t: The ARP verification flow consists of four phases:

dl.dd: The domain owner generates an Ed25519 keypair and publishes the public key as a DNS TXT record under a well-known subdomain: <selector>._arp.<domain>
The domain owner constructs a reasoning payload containing entity claims, identity metadata, and content policies conforming to the ARP JSON Schema.
The payload is canonicalized using JCS , and the canonical form is signed with the Ed25519 private key. The resulting signature is embedded in the payload's _arp_signature block.

A consuming AI agent retrieves the reasoning payload, extracts the DNS selector from the _arp_signature block, queries the corresponding DNS TXT record for the public key, and validates the Ed25519 signature against the JCS-canonicalized payload (excluding the _arp_signature block).

section.name: Record Name
section.t: The DNS TXT record MUST be published at:

where <selector> is a case-insensitive ASCII string matching the pattern [a-zA-Z0-9._-]+ and <domain> is the fully qualified domain name of the entity. The default selector value is "arp", yielding the record name:

section.name: Record Value
section.t: The TXT record value MUST be a semicolon-delimited string of tag=value pairs. The following tags are defined:

tr.td: Base64-encoded raw Ed25519 public key (32 bytes, 44 characters encoded).

section.t: Example:

section.name: Multiple Keys
section.t: A domain MAY publish multiple ARP TXT records using different selectors (e.g., "arp" and "arp2026q3"). This supports key rotation without service interruption.

section.name: Key Size Constraints
section.t: Ed25519 public keys are 32 bytes (44 characters in Base64). This fits within the 255-byte DNS TXT record string limit defined in Section 3.3.14.

section.name: File Location and Discovery
section.t: The reasoning payload SHOULD be served at the well-known URL:
Implementations MAY use alternative paths provided the URL is discoverable via one of the following mechanisms:

ul.li: A <link> element in the HTML head: <link rel="reasoning" href="/path/to/reasoning.json">
A reference in the site's /llms.txt file.

section.t: The file MUST be served with Content-Type: application/json.

section.name: Required Fields
section.t: The reasoning payload MUST contain the following top-level fields:

tr.td: MUST reference the ARP JSON Schema .
Canonical name of the entity (max 200 chars).
MUST contain at minimum a "framing_context" string.

section.name: Optional Sections
section.t: The payload MAY include:

ul.li: identity: Brand identity, tagline, core competencies, emotional resonance attributes, and disambiguation entries ("not_to_be_confused_with").
corrections: Verified factual corrections for known AI hallucinations about this entity, each with trigger_topic, verified_fact, evidence_url, and epistemic_scope.
verification: Audit metadata including auditor identity and timestamps.
authority: Trust signals including Wikipedia, Wikidata, LinkedIn, Crunchbase URIs, awards, and certifications.
content_policy: AI training permissions (allowed, allowed-with-attribution, disallowed, conditional), citation requirements, and verification contact.
diagnostics: RAG pipeline telemetry tokens for ingestion auditing, with transparency statement.

section.name: Canonicalization
section.t: Before signing, the payload MUST be canonicalized according to the JSON Canonicalization Scheme (JCS) as specified in .
The _arp_signature block MUST be excluded from canonicalization. Implementations MUST:

ol.li: Remove the "_arp_signature" key from the copy.
Serialize using JCS (RFC 8785): keys sorted lexicographically, no whitespace, Unicode normalized per RFC 8785 Section 3.2.2.2.
Encode the result as UTF-8 bytes.

section.name: Signing Procedure
section.t: Given a complete reasoning payload and an Ed25519 private key (32 bytes), the signing procedure is:
The RECOMMENDED signature TTL is 90 days. The Base64url encoding MUST use the URL-safe alphabet defined in Section 5 without padding.

section.name: Verification Procedure
section.t: Given a retrieved reasoning payload and the domain from which it was retrieved, the verification procedure is:

ol.li: Validate sig_block contains all REQUIRED fields
Check: sig_block.expires_at > current_time. If expired: FAIL (SIGNATURE_EXPIRED)
Query DNS TXT record at constructed name
Parse TXT record value; extract p= tag (public key). If no record: FAIL (NO_DNS_KEY)
If result == valid: PASS. If result == invalid: FAIL (INVALID_SIGNATURE)

tr.td: DNS key found, signature valid, not expired.
No DNS TXT record at selector._arp.domain.

section.name: Signature Block Format
section.t: The _arp_signature block is a JSON object embedded in the reasoning payload with the following fields:

tr.td: Selector label for DNS TXT lookup (max 50 chars).
Full DNS record name, e.g., "arp._arp.example.com" (max 253 chars).
Base64url-encoded Ed25519 signature (86 chars without padding).

section.name: Key Rotation
section.t: To rotate keys without service disruption:

ol.li: Publish the new public key under a new DNS selector (e.g., "arp2026q3._arp.example.com").
Re-sign the reasoning payload with the new key, updating dns_selector and dns_record accordingly.
After the DNS TTL of the old record has elapsed, remove the old DNS TXT record.

section.t: Verifiers SHOULD support checking multiple selectors. The RECOMMENDED rotation interval is 90 days, aligned with the default signature TTL.

tr.td: ARP adapts the DNS-anchored key publication model from DKIM for entity claim verification rather than email authentication.
C2PA verifies media provenance via embedded manifests. ARP verifies textual entity claim provenance via DNS. Complementary, non-overlapping scope.
VCs require an Issuer-Holder-Verifier ecosystem. ARP provides a simpler two-party (Publisher-Verifier) model for public entity claims.

AIVS verifies what an agent DID (session/action integrity). ARP verifies what an agent READS (content provenance). Complementary: AIVS audit logs plus ARP content verification form a full end-to-end trust chain for agentic systems.
ARP signed payloads could be registered as SCITT transparency statements for append-only auditability.
Schema.org describes entity attributes. ARP cryptographically binds those attributes to verified domain ownership.

section.name: DNS Security
section.t: ARP's trust anchor is the DNS TXT record. Without DNSSEC, an attacker who can poison DNS responses can substitute a fraudulent public key. Implementations SHOULD use DNSSEC-validated resolvers where available. Implementations SHOULD also cache DNS responses according to the record's TTL to reduce lookup frequency.

section.name: Key Compromise
section.t: If an Ed25519 private key is compromised, the attacker can sign arbitrary payloads that will validate against the DNS-published key. Domain owners MUST:

ul.li: Store private keys with restricted file permissions (0600).
Rotate keys immediately on suspected compromise.
Use the expires_at field to limit the validity window of any signature.
Consider hardware security modules (HSMs) or secure enclaves for high-value domains.

section.name: Replay and Staleness
section.t: Verifiers SHOULD check the expires_at timestamp and reject expired signatures. The RECOMMENDED TTL is 90 days. Verifiers MAY also check the signed_at timestamp against the HTTP Last-Modified header or data_freshness field to detect stale payloads served from cache.

section.name: Content Truthfulness
section.t: ARP verifies PROVENANCE (who authorized the data), not TRUTH (whether the data is factually correct). A domain owner can sign factually incorrect claims that will pass ARP verification. AI systems consuming ARP-verified payloads SHOULD still cross-reference entity claims against independent sources. The epistemic_scope field in entity claims is designed to help AI systems distinguish between publicly verifiable facts and proprietary internal claims.

section.name: Narrative Injection without ARP
section.t: Without ARP verification, any actor can publish reasoning.json files containing arbitrary entity claims on any domain they control. Regulatory frameworks such as the EU AI Act Article 19 require audit trails for high-risk AI systems but do not prescribe verification formats for ingested content. Empirical research has demonstrated that RAG systems will cite well-structured entity claims from previously unknown domains as primary sources within 24 hours, regardless of domain history, traffic, or backlinks. ARP provides the verification layer that allows consuming systems to differentiate between authorized and unauthorized entity claims.

section.name: Cross-Domain Signing
section.t: ARP supports scenarios where an authorized third party (e.g., a GEO consultancy) signs a reasoning payload on behalf of a domain owner. In this case, the dns_record field SHOULD reference the third party's domain, and the payload SHOULD include a cross_verification section with sameAs links to establish the trust relationship. Consuming systems SHOULD treat cross-domain signatures with lower confidence than same-domain signatures.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.
The DNS TXT record subtree "_arp" is used by convention and does not require IANA registration, following the pattern established by DKIM ("_domainkey"), DMARC ("_dmarc"), and MTA-STS ("_mta-sts").

author.organization: Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity

front.title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence

section.name: Reasoning Payload

ol.li: Parse p= tag to obtain public key bytes

section.name: JSON Schema
section.t: The normative JSON Schema for ARP v1.2 payloads is published at:

The schema defines validation rules for all required and optional fields, including type constraints, maximum lengths, enumerated values, and format requirements. Implementations SHOULD validate payloads against the schema before signing and before processing entity claims.

section.name: Acknowledgements
section.t: The author thanks the W3C Agentic Integrity Verification Specification Community Group for their complementary work on agent session integrity, and the broader Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) community for empirical validation of the content provenance gap that ARP addresses.
